analogy: Think of "Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure" through the chapter's own opening images: Scientists are constantly discovering new compounds, orderly arranging the facts about them, trying to explain with the existing knowledge, organising to modify the earlier views or evolve theories for explaining the newly observed facts. Unit 4 After studying this Unit, you will be able to... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that scientists are constantly discovering new compounds, orderly arranging the facts about them, trying to explain with the existing knowledge, organising to modify the earlier views or evolve theories for explaining the newly observed facts.

The text explains that unit 4 After studying this Unit, you will be able to • u n d e r s t a n d K ö s s e l - L e w i s approach to chemical bonding; • explain the octet rule and its limitations, draw Lewis structures of simple molecules; • explain the formation of different types of bonds; • describe the VSEPR theory and predict the geometry of simple molecules; • e x p l a i n t h e v a l e n c e b o n d approach for the formation of covalent bonds; • predict the directional properties of covalent bonds; • explain the different types of hybridisation involving s, p and d orbitals and draw shapes of simple covalent molecules; • describe the molecular orbital theory of homonuclear diatomic molecules; • explain the concept of hydrogen bond.

The text explains that cHEMiCAL BOnDinG AnD MOLECULAR StRUCtURE Matter is made up of one or different type of elements.

The text explains that under normal conditions no other element exists as an independent atom in nature, except noble gases.

The text explains that however, a group of atoms is found to exist together as one species having characteristic properties.

The text explains that obviously there must be some force which holds these constituent atoms together in the molecules. the attractive force which holds various constituents (atoms, ions, etc.) together in different chemical species is called a chemical bond.

The text explains that since the formation of chemical compounds takes place as a result of combination of atoms of various elements in different ways, it raises many questions.

The text explains that why do some atoms combine while certain others do not?

The text explains that to answer such questions different theories and concepts have been put forward from time to time.

The text explains that these are Kössel-Lewis approach, Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) Theory, Valence Bond (VB) Theory and Molecular Orbital (MO) Theory.

The text explains that the evolution of various theories of valence and the interpretation of the nature of chemical bonds have closely been related to the developments in the understanding of the structure of atom, the electronic configuration of elements and the periodic table.

The text explains that every system tends to be more stable and bonding is nature’s way of lowering the energy of the system to attain stability.

The text explains that 101 CHEMI CAL B ONDING AND MOLE CULAR S T R U CTURE 4.1 KÖSSEL-LEwiS AppROACH tO CHEMiCAL BOnDinG In order to explain the formation of chemical bond in terms of electrons, a number of attempts were made, but it was only in 1916 when Kössel and Lewis succeeded independently in giving a satisfactory explanation.

The text explains that they were the first to provide some logical explanation of valence which was based on the inertness of noble gases.

The text explains that lewis pictured the atom in terms of a positively charged ‘Kernel’ (the nucleus plus the inner electrons) and the outer shell that could accommodate a maximum of eight electrons.

The text explains that he, further assumed that these eight electrons occupy the corners of a cube which surround the ‘Kernel’.

The text explains that the crystal structure of sodium chloride, NaCl (rock salt), for example is shown below.

The text explains that rock salt structure In ionic solids, the sum of the electron gain enthalpy and the ionization enthalpy may be positive but still the crystal structure gets stabilized due to the energy released in the formation of the crystal lattice.

The text explains that the solid crystal being three- dimensional; it is not possible to calculate lattice enthalpy directly from the interaction of forces of attraction and repulsion only.

The text explains that in the case of a covalent bond, the contribution from each atom is called the covalent radius of that atom. the covalent radius is measured approximately as the radius of an atom’s core which is in contact with the core of an adjacent atom in a bonded situation.

The text explains that for example the dipole moment of HF may be represented as : This arrow symbolises the direction of the shift of electron density in the molecule.

The text explains that note that the direction of crossed arrow is opposite to the conventional direction of dipole moment vector.

The text explains that this is because the two equal bond dipoles point in opposite directions and cancel the effect of each other.

The text explains that this is because, in case of NH 3 the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the N – H bonds, whereas in NF 3 the orbital dipole is in the direction opposite to the resultant dipole moment of the three N–F bonds.

The text explains that this is precisely what happens in a covalent bond, i.e., buildup of electron charge density between the nuclei.

The text explains that 119 CHEMI CAL B ONDING AND MOLE CULAR S T R U CTURE 4.5.2 Directional properties of Bonds As we have already seen, the covalent bond is formed by overlapping of atomic orbitals.

The text explains that the valence bond theory explains the shape, the formation and directional properties of bonds in polyatomic molecules like CH 4, NH 3 and H2O, etc. in terms of overlap and hybridisation of atomic orbitals.

The text explains that 4.5.3 Overlapping of Atomic Orbitals When orbitals of two atoms come close to form bond, their overlap may be positive, negative or zero depending upon the sign (phase) and direction of orientation of amplitude of orbital wave function in space (Fig.

The text explains that 4.9 show the sign (phase) of orbital wave function and are not related to charge.

The text explains that the 2s orbital of carbon and the 1s orbital of H are spherically symmetrical and they can overlap in any direction.

The text explains that therefore the direction of the fourth C-H bond cannot be ascertained.

The text explains that clearly, it follows that simple atomic orbital overlap does not account for the directional characteristics of bonds in CH4.

The text explains that these hybrid orbitals are directed in space in some preferred direction to have minimum repulsion between electron pairs and thus a stable arrangement.

The text explains that such a molecule in which the central atom is sp-hybridised and linked directly to two other central atoms possesses linear geometry.

The text explains that the two sp hybrids point in the opposite direction along the z-axis with projecting positive lobes and very small negative lobes, which provides more effective overlapping resulting in the formation of stronger bonds.

The text explains that these two sp hybrid orbitals are oriented in opposite direction forming an angle of 180°.

The text explains that the four sp3 hybrid orbitals so formed are directed towards the four corners of the tetrahedron.

The text explains that 125 CHEMI CAL B ONDING AND MOLE CULAR S T R U CTURE Now the five orbitals (i.e., one s, three p and one d orbitals) are available for hybridisation to yield a set of five sp3d hybrid orbitals which are directed towards the five corners of a trigonal bipyramidal as depicted in the Fig.

The text explains that 4.7.1 Formation of Molecular Orbitals Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO) According to wave mechanics, the atomic orbitals can be expressed by wave functions ( ψ ’s) which represent the amplitude of the electron waves.

The text explains that these are obtained from the solution of Schrödinger wave equation.

The text explains that however, since it cannot be solved for any system containing more than one electron, molecular orbitals which are one electron wave functions for molecules are difficult to obtain directly from the solution of Schrödinger wave equation.

The text explains that the atomic orbitals of these atoms may be represented by the wave functions ψA and ψB.

The text explains that mathematically, the formation of molecular orbitals may be described by the linear combination of atomic orbitals that can take place by addition and by subtraction of wave functions of individual atomic orbitals as shown below : ψMO = ψA + ψB Therefore, the two molecular orbitals σ and σ* are formed as : σ = ψA + ψB σ* = ψA – ψB The molecular orbital σ formed by the addition of atomic orbitals is called the bonding molecular orbital while the molecular orbital σ* formed by the subtraction of atomic orbital is called antibonding molecular orbital as depicted in Fig.

The text explains that qualitatively, the formation of molecular orbitals can be understood in terms of the constructive or destructive interference of the electron waves of the combining atoms.

The text explains that in the formation of bonding molecular orbital, the two electron waves of the bonding atoms reinforce each other due to constructive interference while in the formation of σ* = ψA – ψB ψA ψB σ = ψA + ψB

The text explains that 127 ChemiCal Bonding and moleCular StruCture antibonding molecular orbital, the electron waves cancel each other due to destructive interference.

The text explains that as a result, the electron density in a bonding molecular orbital is located between the nuclei of the bonded atoms because of which the repulsion between the nuclei is very less while in case of an antibonding molecular orbital, most of the electron density is located away from the space between the nuclei.

The text explains that infact, there is a nodal plane (on which the electron density is zero) between the nuclei and hence the repulsion between the nuclei is high.

The text explains that greater the extent of overlap, the greater will be the electron-density between the nuclei of a molecular orbital.

The text explains that 4.20(a), page 124]. if internuclear axis is taken to be in the z-direction, it can be seen that a linear combination of 2pz- orbitals of two atoms also produces two sigma molecular orbitals designated as 2p z and *2p z. [Fig.

The text explains that a π bonding MO has larger electron density above and below the inter-nuclear axis.

The text explains that illustrate by giving one exmaple of each type.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4: Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure
